world.title: KENYA: Ahead of 21st AMECEA Plenary Assembly, Catholic Communicators Trained on Ethical Coverage
world.desc:
Sr. Jecinter Antoinette Okoth, FSSA
As Kenya prepares to host the 21st Plenary Assembly of the Association of Member Episcopal Conferences in Eastern Africa (AMECEA), the Kenya Conference of Catholic Bishops (KCCB), through its Commission for Social Communications, in partnership with the Media Council of Kenya (MCK), organized a four-day training aimed to equip media practitioners, to deliver accurate, professional, and impactful coverage of the plenary.
According to the organizers, the training underscores the Church's commitment to promoting excellence in communication and strengthening the capacity of media professionals to tell stories that inspire, inform, and foster unity across the AMECEA region.
Speaking during the opening of the training on Monday, July 6, Fr. Gerald Omunyin, the National Executive Secretary at KCCB’s Commission for Social Communications, described the presence of the media practitioners as evidence of the vital partnership between the Church and the media in communicating the Gospel and serving society through truthful, ethical, and professional journalism.
He noted that the AMECEA Plenary Assembly is one of the most significant gatherings of the Catholic Church in Eastern Africa and will bring together bishops, clergy, religious, lay faithful, and other delegates from across the region to reflect on the life and mission of the Church, discern pastoral priorities, and strengthen the shared commitment to evangelization and human development.
Fr. Omunyin further told the over 30 participants that the success of the Assembly would depend not only on the quality of its deliberations but also on how effectively its message reaches the people of God and the wider public.
This, he said, is a responsibility that places journalists at the centre of the process rather than on its margins.
 You are not merely observers of the event; you are partners in communicating hope, promoting understanding, and ensuring that the fruits of the Assembly reach homes, parishes, institutions, and communities throughout Eastern Africa and beyond, he said.
The Catholic communicators were reminded of the importance of observing ethical journalism standards: verifying information before publication, respecting copyright laws, and maintaining accuracy and credibility in all content produced.
On his part, Fr. Andrew Kaufa, the Communication Coordinator at AMECEA, enlightened the participants, who were from various media houses within the country, on the context, mission, and significance of AMECEA. He narrated to the participants the history of AMECEA, the Church structures, and leadership communication.
All this aimed to equip participants with a deeper understanding of the regional Church, enabling them to report accurately, communicate the Church's mission faithfully, provide meaningful context for Church events and decisions, and foster unity and collaboration among the member Episcopal Conferences.
Additionally, the participants were further encouraged to use podcasts as a platform for civic education, public awareness, and community engagement, particularly among young people, who increasingly consume information through digital media.
Addressing them on Wednesday, July 8, Ms. Christine Nguku, the MCK assistant director of training and curriculum development, emphasized that successful podcasts begin with a clear understanding of the target audience.
Besides, the content creators were further urged to identify their listeners' needs, interests, and challenges to produce informative and engaging episodes, and to promote their podcasts on digital platforms such as Facebook, Instagram, TikTok, YouTube, and Spotify to increase visibility and reach wider audiences. The training explored various ways of monetizing podcasts, including sponsorships, memberships, advertising, and partnerships with organizations seeking to reach specific audiences.

This is especially true for ordinary Russians and observers outs

world.title: ZAMBIA: ZCCB Begins Strategic Planning Process for 2027-2036
world.desc:
Sandra Kunda
The Zambia Conference of Catholic Bishops (ZCCB) has commenced developing its 10-year Strategic Plan for the period 2027–2036, aimed at strengthening the Church’s mission and responding to social, economic, political, and pastoral challenges.
The process has entered a key stage with a Strategic Planning Workshop, held from June 21 to 27 at Kasisi Retreat Centre in Lusaka, bringing together about 45 participants from the Conference and its affiliated institutions.
Speaking during the opening Mass of the Workshop, ZCCB Secretary General Fr. Francis Mukosa emphasized the importance of introspection for both individuals and organizations, saying it helps to recognize areas of success and identify mistakes that require correction.
And in his opening remarks, Fr. Mukosa said the planning process is intended to provide a pastoral vision rooted in the Gospel and guide the Church’s work over the next decade.
Fr. Mukosa noted that the new strategic plan builds on previous planning processes, including the current 2017–2026 Strategic Plan, which sought to promote deeper faith and moral uprightness.
The Secretary General said the Church must continue serving as a moral voice in society by promoting peace, dialogue, reconciliation, and integral human development, while responding to the needs of the poor, young people, and other vulnerable groups.
Fr. Mukosa urged participants to use the workshop as an opportunity for a prayerful discernment and collaboration, expressing confidence that the resulting strategic plan will strengthen evangelization, stewardship, and unity within both the Church and the nation.
The strategic planning process started earlier with two Senior Staff Meetings held at the Catholic Secretariat’s Kapingila Guest House.
The first meeting announced the commencement of the strategic planning process and outlined the roadmap, while the second meeting focused on departmental SWOT (Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, and Threats) analyses, providing departments with an opportunity to assess their performance, identify key priorities, and contribute to the development of the ZCCB 10-Year Strategic Plan.
END

world.title: ZAMBIA: Couples For Christ (CFC) - Solwezi Diocese distributes Holy Rosaries to Children Ahead of Peace, Justice and Unity Prayer Campaign
world.desc:
Fr. Wilbroad Musonda-Solwezi Diocese
Couples For Christ (CFC) - Solwezi Diocese distributed Holy Rosaries to children and elders of St. Cecilia Catholic Church in preparation for the Catholic Aid Organization’s annual Aid to the Church in Need (CAN) One Million Children Praying the Rosary Prayer Campaign. The children will join the Peace, Justice and Unity Prayer Campaign during the Marian month of October 2026.
In a message to the Couples for Christ WhatsApp group recently, CFC Missionary Volunteer Mrs Bing Chavez, who hails from the Philippines and serves as both a Missionary Volunteer and a teacher, stated that the initiative aims to teach children and the lay faithful how to pray the Holy Rosary as part of their daily Christian life.
“The purpose of this initiative, that is, the distribution of Holy Rosaries, was intended to teach and lay out for the faithful how to pray the Holy Rosary in their daily Christian life,” explained Mrs. Chavez. She added that the spiritual formation journey will continue until it culminates in October 2026, the Marian month.
“The spiritual formation journey of praying the Holy Rosary will continue until culminating in the Marian month of October 2026”, said the CFC Missionary Volunteer.
Later, the Holy Rosaries were blessed by the Priest-in-Charge of St. Cecilia Catholic Church, in the spiritual accompaniment and presence of Mr. Joel Chavez, leader of CFC-Solwezi Diocese. The Holy Rosaries were later placed around the children's necks, and the faithful sang a Marian hymn in a solemn yet joyful atmosphere.
Meanwhile, 12-year-old John Mubanda, one of the children who received the Holy Rosary, said the Holy Rosary would help him grow deeper in faith as a member of the Holy Childhood.
“By praying the Holy Rosary, I will be helped to grow deeper in my Christian life,” said Mubanda, of the Pontifical Mission Societies (PMS)-Solwezi Diocese Holy Childhood. The Holy Rosaries were also distributed to children of St. Kizito Parish and St. Augustine Parish situated in the Diocese of Solwezi, Zambia.
The campaign, ‘A Million Children Praying the Rosary for Peace and Unity in the World,’ is organized globally by the Pontifical Foundation Aid to the Church in Need (ACN). Inspired by the words of St. Padre Pio, who believed that “when one million children pray the Rosary, the world will change,” the initiative has grown into a significant spiritual movement that involves schools, parishes, and families across continents.
October is dedicated to the Holy Rosary in the Catholic Church. It is a time for the faithful to pray the Rosary to honor the Blessed Virgin Mary and meditate on the mysteries of Christ’s life.
The annual One Million Children Praying the Rosary Prayer Campaign for Peace, Justice and Unity will take place on the Feast of Our Lady of the Rosary, Wednesday, 7 October 2026.

world.title: MALAWI: MCCB Develops Strategy to Strengthen Household Food Security
world.desc:
Brenard Mwanza
The Malawi Conference of Catholic Bishops (MCCB), through its Catholic Health Commission (CHC), has developed an Integrated Homestead Farming (IHF) Implementation Strategy following a three-day workshop held in Balaka from 8 to 10 July 2026, as part of efforts to improve household food security, nutrition and resilience to climate change.
The workshop brought together representatives from Save the Children International, the Creative Centre for Community Mobilization (CRECCOM), government ministries and departments, and other partners to agree on an evidence-based framework for implementing the program in communities across Mangochi, Machinga, Balaka, Ntcheu, Zomba and Phalombe districts.
Speaking during the workshop, MCCB Director of Social Development, Reverend Father Patrick Kamba, said Malawi's food and nutrition challenges demanded long-term, community-led solutions rather than continued reliance on emergency interventions.
Integrated Homestead Farming is not just about growing food; it is about restoring dignity, improving nutrition and building resilient families, he said.
The strategy is informed by assessments conducted in 60 villages in Balaka, 100 in Machinga, and 59 in Phalombe. The findings showed that while 91.7 percent of communities are moderately prepared to adopt the approach, 8.3 percent require additional support due to limited access to water, agricultural inputs, and other household resources.
The assessments also identified strong local leadership and community participation as key opportunities for successful implementation.
Father Kamba said the findings underscored the need for greater investment in water harvesting, small-scale irrigation, agricultural extension services and market linkages to help communities become more self-reliant.
The assessment shows that our communities have the willingness and local leadership needed to embrace Integrated Homestead Farming, but they require coordinated support to overcome barriers such as water shortages, limited agricultural inputs and household resource constraints, he said.
The workshop, held under the Green Climate Fund-supported Climate Resilient Health and Well-being for Rural Communities (CHWBRC) project, concluded with participants validating the implementation strategy and agreeing on a roadmap for its rollout across the target districts.
END
